techniques.name: Urgency & scarcity framing
techniques.description: Language engineered to pressure fast, unconsidered action.
techniques.signals: Deadline, suspension and reward markers; high imperative density.
techniques.defense: Linguistic lure classifiers; combine with sender reputation rather than keywords alone.

techniques.name: Brand & authority impersonation
techniques.description: Trusted logos, layouts and tone used to borrow credibility.
techniques.signals: Brand assets paired with a mismatched or unauthenticated sender.
techniques.defense: Brand-asset matching, logo detection, sender-to-brand consistency checks.

techniques.name: Obfuscation
techniques.description: Intent is hidden from filters using encoding, invisible characters or image-only bodies.
techniques.signals: High image-to-text ratio, unicode anomalies, zero-width characters.
techniques.defense: Unicode normalisation, optical character recognition, structural analysis.

techniques.name: Content polymorphism
techniques.description: Each recipient receives a slightly different message to defeat exact signatures.
techniques.signals: High template entropy; weak exact-match clustering across a burst.
techniques.defense: Fuzzy hashing and near-duplicate clustering rather than exact signatures.

techniques.name: Redirect & cloaking chains
techniques.description: Links pass through shorteners and multiple hops, sometimes serving different content to analysers than to victims.
techniques.signals: URL shorteners, multi-hop redirects, geo- or user-agent-based cloaking.
techniques.defense: URL detonation in a sandbox, follow chains, analyse final destinations.

techniques.name: Reputation warming
techniques.description: A new IP or domain is ramped up gradually to build trust before abuse.
techniques.signals: Steady volume increase on young infrastructure; benign early sends.
techniques.defense: Model sender tenure and velocity together; treat trust as earned over time.

techniques.name: Send-and-burn
techniques.description: Infrastructure is used intensively for a short window then abandoned.
techniques.signals: Sources active for hours then silent; rapid infrastructure turnover.
techniques.defense: Fast indicator sharing, first-contact caution, short-tenure penalties.

techniques.name: List bombing & subscription abuse
techniques.description: Signup and confirmation flows are abused to flood a target with mail.
techniques.signals: Bursts of subscription-confirmation messages to one recipient.
techniques.defense: Rate limits and challenge mechanisms on signup forms; double-opt-in hygiene.

techniques.name: Locale & timing targeting
techniques.description: Delivery is timed to a victim's business hours and locale to raise engagement.
techniques.signals: Send timing aligned to recipient time zone and working hours.
techniques.defense: Temporal baselining of normal correspondence patterns.

techniques.name: Credential-harvest destinations
techniques.description: Cloned login pages hosted to capture entered credentials.
techniques.signals: Login clones on newly-registered domains or throwaway hosting; brand mismatch.
techniques.defense: Destination reputation, sandbox rendering, credential-form detection.

techniques.name: Weaponised attachments
techniques.description: Documents or archives carrying macros, scripts or executables.
techniques.signals: Macro-enabled documents, risky archive and file types, double extensions.
techniques.defense: Attachment sandboxing, file-type policy, macro restrictions.

techniques.name: Reply funnels
techniques.description: The message invites a reply that moves the victim into a fraud conversation.
techniques.signals: Free-mail reply-to, thread hijacking, reply-to and From divergence.
techniques.defense: Reply-to and From mismatch flags, conversation-thread integrity checks.

techniques.name: Tracking beacons
techniques.description: Invisible images and unique links confirm live, engaged recipients.
techniques.signals: One-pixel images, per-recipient tracking URLs.
techniques.defense: Image proxying and stripping, link rewriting and neutralisation.

techniques.name: Snowshoe distribution
techniques.description: Sending volume is spread thinly across a wide range of IP addresses and domains so no single source crosses a reputation or rate threshold.
techniques.signals: Many low-volume, newly-seen IPs across unrelated netblocks; shared content fingerprints across sources.
techniques.defense: Score reputation at the netblock and ASN level, not just per-IP; baseline expected volume per source.

techniques.name: Botnet / compromised hosts
techniques.description: Mail originates from large numbers of compromised end-user machines.
techniques.signals: Residential or dynamic IP ranges, missing or generic reverse DNS, inconsistent HELO identities.
techniques.defense: Apply policy-block and dynamic-user lists; treat residential-origin mail with caution.

techniques.name: Compromised account abuse
techniques.description: A legitimate mailbox or tenant is taken over and used to send.
techniques.signals: Sudden change in sending behaviour, geolocation or velocity anomalies, unusual send times.
techniques.defense: Authentication-anomaly detection, adaptive rate limits, session and device baselining.

techniques.name: ESP / bulk-provider abuse
techniques.description: Legitimate email-service providers are used to distribute abusive lists.
techniques.signals: Valid provider headers paired with abusive content or unconsented recipients.
techniques.defense: Honour feedback loops, track reputation per campaign and per sub-account, complaint-rate monitoring.

techniques.name: Hijacked infrastructure
techniques.description: Dormant subdomains, dangling DNS records or routing weaknesses are abused to send authenticated-looking mail.
techniques.signals: Authenticated mail from long-inactive subdomains; records pointing at unclaimed resources.
techniques.defense: Routine DNS hygiene, retire dangling records, use DMARC reporting for visibility.

techniques.name: Exact-domain spoofing
techniques.description: A real domain is forged in the From header where authentication is absent or unenforced.
techniques.signals: SPF or DKIM failure with a DMARC policy of none; envelope and header mismatch.
techniques.defense: Enforce DMARC at quarantine then reject; align SPF and DKIM. (See the SpamCrackers Standard.)

techniques.name: Lookalike / cousin domains
techniques.description: Domains that visually or typographically resemble a trusted brand.
techniques.signals: Recently registered homoglyph or typo variants; low domain age; brand terms in the label.
techniques.defense: Brand and lookalike monitoring, homoglyph normalisation, newly-registered-domain scoring.

techniques.name: Display-name impersonation
techniques.description: The friendly display name claims a trusted identity while the real address does not match.
techniques.signals: Friendly-From to envelope-From mismatch; free-mail address behind a corporate display name.
techniques.defense: Header-From analysis, first-contact and external-sender warning banners.

techniques.name: Newly-registered-domain sending
techniques.description: Domains registered shortly before a campaign, with no reputation to lose.
techniques.signals: Domain age below a small threshold; registrar and nameserver clustering.
techniques.defense: Age-based scoring and short-lived greylisting for very new domains.

techniques.name: Authentication-passing abuse
techniques.description: Mail that legitimately passes SPF/DKIM/DMARC yet is still abusive.
techniques.signals: Aligned authentication combined with abusive content, lists or behaviour.
techniques.defense: Reputation and content analysis beyond authentication; authentication proves identity, not intent.

techniques.name: Affiliate & lead resale
techniques.description: Clicks and leads are monetised through commission networks.
techniques.signals: Affiliate tracking parameters, redirection to marketing funnels.
techniques.defense: Destination and network reputation, funnel fingerprinting.

techniques.name: Advance-fee & romance fraud
techniques.description: Long-form social engineering aimed at direct payment.
techniques.signals: Emotional narratives, requests to move to private channels.
techniques.defense: Narrative classifiers, reply-path analysis, user education.

techniques.name: Invoice & payment fraud
techniques.description: Fake or altered invoices and payment-redirection requests (bridges to the BEC pillar).
techniques.signals: Payment-detail changes, urgency around transfers, vendor impersonation.
techniques.defense: Out-of-band verification of payment changes, vendor allow-lists.

techniques.name: Credential & data resale
techniques.description: Harvested credentials and data are sold or reused downstream.
techniques.signals: Post-compromise access from harvesting campaigns.
techniques.defense: Credential-reuse monitoring, forced resets, breach correlation.

techniques.name: Extortion
techniques.description: Threats and coercion, including mass sextortion, demanding payment.
techniques.signals: Threatening templates, cryptocurrency payment demands, mass identical sends.
techniques.defense: Template clustering, payment-indicator extraction, awareness messaging.

techniques.name: Cloned login page
techniques.description: A pixel-accurate fake sign-in page records whatever the victim enters.
techniques.signals: A credential form served from a non-official domain.
techniques.defense: Password managers withhold autofill off-domain; verify the domain.

techniques.name: Adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM)
techniques.description: A live reverse proxy relays to the real site, capturing the authenticated session and defeating many MFA methods.
techniques.signals: Reverse-proxy patterns and real-time token relay.
techniques.defense: Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/passkeys), token binding, conditional access.

techniques.name: OAuth consent phishing
techniques.description: The victim is tricked into granting an application permission — no password is ever needed.
techniques.signals: Consent prompts from unverified or over-scoped applications.
techniques.defense: Application governance, restrict user consent, review granted scopes.

techniques.name: OTP interception
techniques.description: The victim is prompted to relay a one-time code in real time so the attacker can complete sign-in.
techniques.signals: A page requesting an OTP immediately after a password.
techniques.defense: Phishing-resistant MFA; educate users never to relay codes.

techniques.name: Progressive data harvesting
techniques.description: Staged forms collect more than credentials — personal, financial or recovery data.
techniques.signals: Multi-step forms requesting escalating personal information.
techniques.defense: Detect multi-step credential-and-data form flows.

techniques.name: HTML smuggling
techniques.description: The payload is assembled inside the browser from a benign-looking HTML file to bypass gateway scanning.
techniques.signals: HTML attachments that build content or downloads client-side.
techniques.defense: Sandbox rendering, restrict risky attachment types.

techniques.name: QR-code phishing (quishing)
techniques.description: The link is delivered as a QR image, pushing the victim onto a less-protected personal device.
techniques.signals: An image-heavy message with a QR code and little text.
techniques.defense: Extract and analyse URLs from QR images; brief staff on the tactic.

techniques.name: Redirect & cloaking chains
techniques.description: Multi-hop redirection serves different content to analysers than to victims (shared with SPM-DE-01).
techniques.signals: Shorteners, multi-hop redirects, geo- or user-agent-based cloaking.
techniques.defense: Detonate URLs and follow chains from multiple vantage points.

techniques.name: Link obfuscation
techniques.description: The true destination is hidden behind encoding or lookalike URLs.
techniques.signals: Encoded URLs, or visible text that disagrees with the underlying link.
techniques.defense: URL normalisation, expose the real href before allowing the click.

techniques.name: Sandbox evasion
techniques.description: CAPTCHA, geo or time gating makes analysers see benign content while victims see the lure.
techniques.signals: Content that differs between an automated analyser and a real user.
techniques.defense: Realistic detonation environments and repeat analysis over time.

techniques.name: Lookalike / cousin domains
techniques.description: Domains that resemble a trusted brand by homoglyph or typo (bridges SPM-ID-02).
techniques.signals: Recently registered similar domains; brand terms in the label.
techniques.defense: Brand and lookalike monitoring, homoglyph normalisation.

techniques.name: Compromised legitimate hosting
techniques.description: Landing pages are hosted on hacked but reputable websites to inherit their trust.
techniques.signals: A login clone on an unrelated, otherwise-reputable domain path.
techniques.defense: Destination reputation and content inspection, not domain trust alone.

techniques.name: Newly-registered domains
techniques.description: Domains registered shortly before use, with no reputation to lose.
techniques.signals: Very low domain age; registrar and nameserver clustering.
techniques.defense: Age-based scoring and short-lived greylisting for new domains.

techniques.name: Free hosting & redirectors
techniques.description: Legitimate free platforms and open redirectors are abused to host or forward to the payload.
techniques.signals: Final destination on free-host subdomains or via open redirects.
techniques.defense: Expand and inspect full redirect chains to the true endpoint.

techniques.name: TLS-for-trust
techniques.description: A valid certificate is obtained so the destination shows a padlock and looks legitimate.
techniques.signals: A brand-new certificate on a lookalike or throwaway domain.
techniques.defense: Treat TLS as encryption, not identity; monitor certificate transparency.

techniques.name: Compromised sender account
techniques.description: Messages are sent from a real, trusted mailbox that has been taken over (bridges SPM-IA-03).
techniques.signals: Behavioural anomalies on an otherwise-trusted internal or partner account.
techniques.defense: Authentication-anomaly detection, adaptive rate limits.

techniques.name: Brand impersonation
techniques.description: Trusted brand identity, layout and tone are cloned to borrow credibility.
techniques.signals: Brand assets paired with a mismatched or unauthenticated sender.
techniques.defense: Brand-to-sender consistency checks, logo detection.

techniques.name: Authority & urgency
techniques.description: Pressure from a claimed authority pushes fast, unconsidered action.
techniques.signals: Deadline, suspension or executive-tone markers with an external link.
techniques.defense: Verify unusual requests through a separate, trusted channel.

techniques.name: Account-security pretext
techniques.description: A message claims the account is at risk and must be “verified” now.
techniques.signals: Security-alert templates driving to an external sign-in link.
techniques.defense: Never authenticate through emailed links; navigate directly.

techniques.name: Payment & invoice pretext
techniques.description: Fake invoices, receipts or payment-detail changes (bridges the BEC pillar).
techniques.signals: Invoice or banking-change requests, especially with urgency.
techniques.defense: Out-of-band verification of any payment change.

techniques.name: Reply-chain hijacking
techniques.description: The lure is inserted into a genuine, existing email thread to inherit its trust.
techniques.signals: An unexpected reply resuming an old thread with a new link or file.
techniques.defense: Scrutinise links and attachments even within known threads.

techniques.name: Account takeover
techniques.description: Captured credentials or sessions are used to seize the account.
techniques.signals: Anomalous logins shortly after a campaign.
techniques.defense: Session revocation, conditional access, impossible-travel detection.

techniques.name: BEC pivot
techniques.description: A trusted mailbox is used to run financial fraud from the inside (bridges the BEC pillar).
techniques.signals: Internal payment or vendor requests from a genuine account.
techniques.defense: Out-of-band verification of payment and vendor changes.

techniques.name: Mailbox persistence
techniques.description: Forwarding rules and OAuth tokens are planted for durable, quiet access.
techniques.signals: New auto-forwarding rules or unfamiliar OAuth grants.
techniques.defense: Audit inbox rules and tokens, alert on new grants.

techniques.name: Data exfiltration
techniques.description: Mail and files are harvested from the compromised account.
techniques.signals: Bulk access or download activity after compromise.
techniques.defense: Data-loss prevention and access-anomaly detection.

techniques.name: Onward propagation
techniques.description: The victim's own contacts are phished from within the trusted account.
techniques.signals: Internal phishing originating from a trusted mailbox.
techniques.defense: Internal-phish detection and rapid account containment.

techniques.name: OSINT profiling
techniques.description: Public information is gathered to select and tailor a convincing approach to a specific person or team.
techniques.signals: Lures referencing niche, publicly-available detail about the recipient or their role.
techniques.defense: Reduce unnecessary public exposure of staff detail; brief high-risk roles.

techniques.name: Address harvesting
techniques.description: Valid recipient addresses are collected from leaks, scraping or guessing patterns.
techniques.signals: Delivery to scraped, generated or rarely-used addresses.
techniques.defense: Address aliasing, breach-exposure monitoring, catch-all hygiene.

techniques.name: Org-structure mapping
techniques.description: Reporting lines and relationships are mapped to make impersonation credible.
techniques.signals: Impersonation of a specific internal role or a known supplier contact.
techniques.defense: Establish internal verification norms for unusual requests.

techniques.name: Event-timed targeting
techniques.description: Campaigns are timed to payroll runs, tax season, outages or corporate events.
techniques.signals: A lure aligned suspiciously well to a current internal or public event.
techniques.defense: Heighten scrutiny during known high-risk windows; pre-warn staff.

techniques.name: Internal account compromise
techniques.description: An internal mailbox is taken over to send from a genuine identity (bridges PHI-CC-02).
techniques.signals: Anomalous login or behaviour on a finance or executive account.
techniques.defense: Phishing-resistant MFA, conditional access, session monitoring.

techniques.name: Vendor email compromise (VEC)
techniques.description: A supplier's real mailbox is compromised and used against its customers.
techniques.signals: A banking-change request from an otherwise-genuine vendor account.
techniques.defense: Verify vendor changes out-of-band via a pre-known contact.

techniques.name: Lookalike domain
techniques.description: A cousin domain of the company or vendor is registered (bridges SPM-ID-02).
techniques.signals: A near-miss domain in the from or reply-to address.
techniques.defense: Lookalike monitoring and external-sender banners.

techniques.name: Display-name impersonation
techniques.description: Only the friendly name of an executive or vendor is forged (bridges SPM-ID-03).
techniques.signals: A known name paired with an unfamiliar address.
techniques.defense: Header-from analysis and first-contact flags.

techniques.name: Mailbox-rule persistence
techniques.description: Inbox rules hide replies and alerts on a compromised account (bridges PHI-OB-03).
techniques.signals: New auto-forward, move or delete rules.
techniques.defense: Audit inbox rules and alert on rule creation.

techniques.name: Money-mule routing
techniques.description: Funds pass through intermediary accounts to break the trail.
techniques.signals: Payments to newly-added or unusual beneficiaries.
techniques.defense: Beneficiary risk screening and rapid recall procedures.

techniques.name: Rapid layering
techniques.description: Funds are moved quickly across accounts and borders.
techniques.signals: Fast onward transfers immediately after receipt.
techniques.defense: Real-time transaction monitoring and fast bank engagement.

techniques.name: Evidence removal
techniques.description: Sent items, rules and traces are deleted on compromised accounts.
techniques.signals: Missing sent mail or altered inbox rules.
techniques.defense: Immutable logging and out-of-band forensics.

techniques.name: Continued impersonation
techniques.description: Reassurance is used to stall discovery while funds clear.
techniques.signals: Repeated claims that a payment is “processing”.
techniques.defense: Independent confirmation with the real counterparty.

techniques.name: Executive request
techniques.description: A supposed executive asks finance to act urgently and quietly.
techniques.signals: An unusual executive payment request combining urgency and secrecy.
techniques.defense: Mandatory independent verification for executive payment requests.

techniques.name: Vendor bank-change request
techniques.description: A supposed supplier updates their payment details.
techniques.signals: A banking-detail change requested over email.
techniques.defense: Verify every change via a previously-known phone number.

techniques.name: Fraudulent invoice submission
techniques.description: A fake or altered invoice is introduced into the process.
techniques.signals: A new payee, altered account or mismatched totals.
techniques.defense: Three-way match and payee allow-lists.

techniques.name: Thread insertion
techniques.description: The request is placed inside a genuine ongoing conversation.
techniques.signals: A banking change appearing mid a real thread.
techniques.defense: Re-verify any change regardless of the thread it arrives in.

techniques.name: Confidentiality framing
techniques.description: “Keep this between us” is used to block a second opinion.
techniques.signals: Requests for secrecy around a payment.
techniques.defense: Policy that no legitimate payment ever requires secrecy.

techniques.name: Wire-transfer instruction
techniques.description: A direct instruction to send funds to a specified account.
techniques.signals: A first-time payee, urgency and a changed process.
techniques.defense: Dual authorisation and callback verification for transfers.

techniques.name: Banking-detail alteration
techniques.description: Account numbers on a real payment are changed.
techniques.signals: Last-minute account changes before a payment run.
techniques.defense: Verify all changes out-of-band; lock vendor bank data.

techniques.name: Payroll diversion
techniques.description: An employee's direct-deposit destination is redirected.
techniques.signals: Direct-deposit change requests arriving by email.
techniques.defense: Authenticated self-service changes with confirmation.

techniques.name: Gift-card request
techniques.description: Prepaid cards are requested as an untraceable payout.
techniques.signals: An executive asking staff to buy gift cards.
techniques.defense: Policy banning gift-card purchases requested by email.

techniques.name: Verification suppression
techniques.description: Any second channel is discouraged or blocked.
techniques.signals: Excuses for why a confirming phone call cannot happen.
techniques.defense: Make out-of-band verification mandatory, never optional.

techniques.name: Thread-context harvesting
techniques.description: Existing threads are read to mimic tone and reference real context.
techniques.signals: Messages citing accurate, non-public internal detail.
techniques.defense: Treat anomalies as possible compromise; segment sensitive threads.

techniques.name: Pending-payment discovery
techniques.description: Invoices and payments in flight are located so a real one can be hijacked.
techniques.signals: A genuine invoice quietly redirected or altered.
techniques.defense: Verify banking details on every payment, every time.

techniques.name: Signature & tone cloning
techniques.description: Style, signature and disclaimers are replicated for authenticity.
techniques.signals: A near-perfect stylistic match with a subtle address change.
techniques.defense: Never treat tone as identity; verify out-of-band.

techniques.name: Opportune-moment monitoring
techniques.description: Travel, out-of-office or absence is awaited so requests go unchallenged.
techniques.signals: A request timed to a known absence of the real person.
techniques.defense: Delegation and verification norms that persist during absences.

techniques.name: Finance-role identification
techniques.description: Staff able to move money — accounts payable, finance, executives — are pinpointed.
techniques.signals: Targeting or impersonation focused on payment-authorised roles.
techniques.defense: Extra controls and training for anyone who can authorise or execute payments.

techniques.name: Relationship mapping
techniques.description: Trust relationships (executive-to-assistant, company-to-vendor) are mapped to make requests credible.
techniques.signals: Impersonation exploiting one specific, known relationship.
techniques.defense: Codified verification norms for relationship-based payment requests.

techniques.name: Transaction-timing research
techniques.description: Requests are aligned to genuinely expected payments or deals.
techniques.signals: A request coinciding suspiciously with a real, pending transaction.
techniques.defense: Dual control and out-of-band checks on every payment and change.

techniques.name: Financial-calendar awareness
techniques.description: Month-end, quarter-end, payroll and tax windows are chosen for pressure.
techniques.signals: Pressure timed precisely to a financial deadline.
techniques.defense: Heightened scrutiny during known high-risk financial windows.

techniques.name: Attachment delivery
techniques.description: The payload rides directly in the message as a file.
techniques.signals: Risky file types arriving as attachments.
techniques.defense: Attachment sandboxing and type policy at the gateway.

techniques.name: Link to hosted payload
techniques.description: A link leads to a download rather than carrying the file.
techniques.signals: Links to direct downloads, especially of risky types.
techniques.defense: URL detonation and download inspection before delivery.

techniques.name: Cloud-service staging
techniques.description: The file is hosted on a reputable cloud service to borrow its trust.
techniques.signals: Payloads staged on file-sharing or cloud platforms.
techniques.defense: Detonate cloud-hosted links; reputation is not safety.

techniques.name: Reply-chain delivery
techniques.description: The lure is inserted into a genuine thread to raise trust (bridges PHI-LP-05).
techniques.signals: An unexpected attachment or link resuming a real thread.
techniques.defense: Scan links and files even within known conversations.

techniques.name: Malvertising redirect
techniques.description: Delivery arrives via an advertising or redirect network to the payload.
techniques.signals: Redirect chains terminating in a download.
techniques.defense: Follow and detonate full redirect chains.

techniques.name: Sandbox evasion
techniques.description: Execution is withheld when analysis environments are detected.
techniques.signals: Content that behaves differently for an analyser than a user.
techniques.defense: Realistic detonation environments and delayed re-analysis.

techniques.name: Packing & obfuscation
techniques.description: Code is compressed or scrambled to defeat static signatures.
techniques.signals: High-entropy or obfuscated files with little static detail.
techniques.defense: Behavioural analysis and unpacking rather than signatures alone.

techniques.name: Password gating
techniques.description: An archive password in the email keeps gateway scanners out of the payload.
techniques.signals: Mailed passwords accompanying archives.
techniques.defense: Extract with the supplied password and inspect; flag the pattern.

techniques.name: Geo & time gating
techniques.description: The payload serves only to certain regions or times to dodge analysis.
techniques.signals: Content varying by geography or hour of request.
techniques.defense: Analyse from multiple vantage points and over time.

techniques.name: Benign-first staging
techniques.description: Early stages appear harmless; capability is fetched only later.
techniques.signals: Innocuous droppers with delayed network activity.
techniques.defense: Egress monitoring and staged-behaviour detection.

techniques.name: Macro execution
techniques.description: Enabled macros run the fetch-and-execute logic.
techniques.signals: Documents spawning script interpreters or network calls.
techniques.defense: Block internet-macro execution; monitor office-to-child processes.

techniques.name: Script-host execution
techniques.description: A shortcut or script triggers an interpreter to run the payload.
techniques.signals: Script hosts launched from mail-delivered files.
techniques.defense: Constrain or disable script hosts; application control.

techniques.name: Document exploit
techniques.description: A viewer vulnerability runs code without user action.
techniques.signals: Crafted documents targeting known viewer flaws.
techniques.defense: Timely patching and exploit-mitigation controls.

techniques.name: User-run persuasion
techniques.description: Social engineering convinces the user to run the file themselves.
techniques.signals: Instructions coaching the user to open or enable.
techniques.defense: User awareness plus application allow-listing.

techniques.name: Living-off-the-land
techniques.description: Trusted system binaries are abused to execute or fetch the payload.
techniques.signals: Unusual use of legitimate system tools.
techniques.defense: Behavioural detection of anomalous native-tool use.

techniques.name: Dropper / loader stage
techniques.description: A first stage installs and launches the main payload.
techniques.signals: Small executables fetching larger second stages.
techniques.defense: Endpoint detection and egress controls on new processes.

techniques.name: Autostart persistence
techniques.description: Registry keys or scheduled tasks re-launch the payload after reboot.
techniques.signals: New autostart entries or scheduled tasks.
techniques.defense: Monitor persistence locations; baseline autostart state.

techniques.name: DLL side-loading
techniques.description: A trusted application is made to load a malicious library.
techniques.signals: Legitimate binaries loading unexpected libraries.
techniques.defense: Application control and library-load monitoring.

techniques.name: Defence tampering
techniques.description: Security tooling is disabled or blinded to protect the foothold.
techniques.signals: Attempts to stop or exclude security agents.
techniques.defense: Tamper protection and independent, off-host alerting.

techniques.name: Data theft
techniques.description: Credentials, cookies, files and secrets are exfiltrated.
techniques.signals: Bulk data access and unusual outbound transfer.
techniques.defense: Data-loss prevention and egress anomaly detection.

techniques.name: Ransomware impact
techniques.description: Data is encrypted or stolen for extortion.
techniques.signals: Mass file changes and shadow-copy deletion.
techniques.defense: Tested backups, segmentation and rapid isolation.

techniques.name: Remote control
techniques.description: An operator gains interactive command of the host.
techniques.signals: Command-and-control beacons and remote sessions.
techniques.defense: Network detection and command-and-control blocking.

techniques.name: Lateral movement
techniques.description: The foothold is used to reach further systems.
techniques.signals: Internal reconnaissance and credential reuse.
techniques.defense: Segmentation, least privilege and identity monitoring.

techniques.name: Onward propagation
techniques.description: The host is used to send further malicious mail (bridges SPM-IA-02).
techniques.signals: Outbound malware or spam from the compromised host.
techniques.defense: Outbound filtering and rapid containment.

techniques.name: Direct executable attachment
techniques.description: An executable is attached, often disguised by icon or double extension.
techniques.signals: Executable or double-extension files attached to mail.
techniques.defense: Block executable attachment types at the gateway by policy.

techniques.name: Macro-enabled document
techniques.description: An office document carries macros that fetch or run the payload when enabled.
techniques.signals: Macro-enabled formats prompting the user to enable content.
techniques.defense: Disable macros from the internet by policy; sandbox documents.

techniques.name: Shortcut & script files
techniques.description: LNK shortcuts or script files (JS, HTA, VBS) invoke an interpreter to run code.
techniques.signals: Shortcut or scripting file types delivered by mail.
techniques.defense: Restrict script hosts and block risky file types.

techniques.name: HTML smuggling package
techniques.description: A benign-looking HTML file assembles the payload in the browser to bypass gateways.
techniques.signals: HTML attachments that build a download client-side.
techniques.defense: Render attachments in a sandbox; restrict HTML attachments.

techniques.name: Password-protected archive
techniques.description: The payload is placed in an archive whose password is given in the email body, keeping scanners out.
techniques.signals: An archive with the password supplied in the message.
techniques.defense: Inspect archive contents; treat mailed archive passwords as a red flag.

techniques.name: Mule networks
techniques.description: Funds pass through recruited intermediaries.
techniques.signals: Payments routed via unrelated personal accounts.
techniques.defense: Beneficiary screening and rapid recall procedures.

techniques.name: Crypto laundering
techniques.description: Cryptocurrency is layered to obscure the trail.
techniques.signals: Rapid movement across wallets and mixers.
techniques.defense: On-chain analytics and fast reporting.

techniques.name: Identity churn
techniques.description: Personas, addresses and accounts are cycled to evade blocks.
techniques.signals: Recurring scripts from ever-changing identities.
techniques.defense: Cluster campaigns by template and behaviour, not identity.

techniques.name: Rising fees
techniques.description: New obstacles justify ever-larger payments.
techniques.signals: A sequence of growing 'final' fees.
techniques.defense: Recognise the sunk-cost trap; stop and report.

techniques.name: Sunk-cost pressure
techniques.description: Prior losses are used to push further payment.
techniques.signals: Appeals to protect what was already paid.
techniques.defense: Past loss never justifies new payment.

techniques.name: Reload targeting
techniques.description: A prior victim is re-approached, sometimes as a fake 'recovery' service.
techniques.signals: Follow-up offers to recover lost funds — for a fee.
techniques.defense: Treat unsolicited recovery offers as second-stage fraud.

techniques.name: Mule recruitment
techniques.description: The victim is enlisted to move others' stolen funds.
techniques.signals: Requests to receive and forward money.
techniques.defense: Awareness that money-handling requests enable laundering.

techniques.name: Advance-fee demand
techniques.description: Fees or taxes are required before the promised payout.
techniques.signals: Payment demanded to release a larger sum.
techniques.defense: No legitimate payout requires an upfront fee.

techniques.name: Payment-detail capture
techniques.description: Bank or card details are solicited directly.
techniques.signals: Requests for full card or banking data by email.
techniques.defense: Never share payment details via unsolicited mail.

techniques.name: Crypto transfer
techniques.description: Irreversible cryptocurrency payment is requested.
techniques.signals: Wallet addresses and urgency to transfer.
techniques.defense: Treat crypto demands as a strong fraud indicator.

techniques.name: Gift-card demand
techniques.description: Prepaid card codes are requested as payment.
techniques.signals: Requests to buy cards and share codes.
techniques.defense: No legitimate body is paid in gift cards.

techniques.name: Data harvest
techniques.description: Identity or account data is collected for later fraud.
techniques.signals: Requests for identity documents or credentials.
techniques.defense: Minimise data sharing; verify the requester.

techniques.name: Windfall claim
techniques.description: An unexpected prize, inheritance or refund is dangled.
techniques.signals: Notification of winnings the recipient never entered.
techniques.defense: Treat unsolicited windfalls as fraud by default.

techniques.name: Romance opener
techniques.description: A friendly or affectionate first contact begins a relationship con.
techniques.signals: Unsolicited personal approaches steering off-platform.
techniques.defense: Awareness of romance-fraud patterns; verify identities.

techniques.name: Investment pitch
techniques.description: A high-return scheme or platform is offered.
techniques.signals: Guaranteed or outsized returns with urgency.
techniques.defense: Independent verification of any investment solicitation.

techniques.name: Job / task offer
techniques.description: An easy, well-paid role is offered, often as a mule funnel.
techniques.signals: Unsolicited offers involving receiving or forwarding money.
techniques.defense: Verify employers; flag money-handling 'jobs'.

techniques.name: Threat / extortion
techniques.description: Fear is used — claimed compromise, debt or legal action.
techniques.signals: Payment demands under threat, often to crypto.
techniques.defense: Recognise extortion templates; never pay, report.

techniques.name: Mass blast
techniques.description: A generic lure is sent at high volume to unfiltered recipients.
techniques.signals: Identical or lightly-varied mail across many unrelated inboxes.
techniques.defense: Bulk-pattern detection and sender reputation.

techniques.name: Targeted list
techniques.description: A list tied to a theme (investors, jobseekers) is used for relevance.
techniques.signals: Lures aligned to a specific audience or interest.
techniques.defense: Context-aware filtering and user awareness for at-risk groups.

techniques.name: Topical & seasonal
techniques.description: Current events, holidays or disasters are used to raise plausibility.
techniques.signals: A lure closely tracking a news event or season.
techniques.defense: Heightened scrutiny of event-themed solicitations.

techniques.name: Authority / brand veneer
techniques.description: A trusted institution or brand is invoked to open the door.
techniques.signals: Institutional branding paired with an unofficial sender.
techniques.defense: Brand-to-sender consistency checks.

techniques.name: Channel migration
techniques.description: The victim is moved to a private channel away from oversight.
techniques.signals: Early pressure to move to chat or personal email.
techniques.defense: Awareness that off-platform moves reduce protection.

techniques.name: Fabricated proof
techniques.description: Fake documents, screenshots or credentials are shown.
techniques.signals: Unverifiable 'evidence' supporting the story.
techniques.defense: Independently verify any document or claim.

techniques.name: Small reciprocity
techniques.description: A tiny gift or early 'return' builds false confidence.
techniques.signals: A small payout preceding a larger request.
techniques.defense: Recognise reciprocity as a manipulation lever.

techniques.name: Urgency & scarcity
techniques.description: A closing window pressures fast, unconsidered action.
techniques.signals: Deadlines attached to money movement.
techniques.defense: Slow down; legitimate opportunities survive verification.

techniques.name: Missing-SPF exploitation
techniques.description: A domain without SPF offers no policy to fail against.
techniques.signals: Forged mail from domains publishing no SPF.
techniques.defense: Publish SPF ending in -all. (See the SpamCrackers Standard.)

techniques.name: Missing-DKIM exploitation
techniques.description: Without DKIM, receivers cannot verify a signature.
techniques.signals: Unsigned mail claiming a brand identity.
techniques.defense: Enable DKIM signing and publish the key.

techniques.name: DMARC-none exploitation
techniques.description: A monitoring-only policy tells receivers to block nothing.
techniques.signals: Spoofed mail passing because DMARC is p=none.
techniques.defense: Enforce DMARC at quarantine, then reject.

techniques.name: Alignment gaps
techniques.description: Authentication passes for a domain other than the visible From.
techniques.signals: SPF or DKIM aligned to an unrelated domain.
techniques.defense: Require DMARC alignment, not just an SPF or DKIM pass.

techniques.name: Forwarder-gap abuse
techniques.description: Legitimate forwarding that breaks SPF is exploited to slip spoofed mail.
techniques.signals: Spoofed mail arriving via forwarding paths.
techniques.defense: Use DKIM and ARC; account for forwarding in policy.

techniques.name: Logo & layout cloning
techniques.description: Brand assets and layout are reproduced to appear genuine.
techniques.signals: Brand assets paired with a mismatched sender.
techniques.defense: Brand-to-sender consistency and logo detection.

techniques.name: Trust-seal misuse
techniques.description: Security badges or seals are shown to imply legitimacy.
techniques.signals: Trust imagery on an unauthenticated message.
techniques.defense: Treat in-message seals as decoration, not proof.

techniques.name: TLS-for-trust
techniques.description: A valid certificate on a fake domain lends a false sense of safety.
techniques.signals: A padlock on a lookalike or new domain.
techniques.defense: TLS is encryption, not identity; check the domain.

techniques.name: Trusted-service abuse
techniques.description: A genuinely reputable platform is used to relay the impersonation.
techniques.signals: Impersonation relayed through a trusted service.
techniques.defense: Inspect content and intent, not only the relay's reputation.

techniques.name: Reputation borrowing
techniques.description: Established infrastructure or services carry the forged mail.
techniques.signals: Impersonation from otherwise-reputable sources.
techniques.defense: Reputation plus alignment, not reputation alone.

techniques.name: Mixed-legitimacy content
techniques.description: Real links and text surround the forged intent to appear benign.
techniques.signals: Mostly-legitimate content with a single forged element.
techniques.defense: Evaluate the whole message and its authentication.

techniques.name: Selective targeting
techniques.description: Only chosen recipients receive the forged mail to limit exposure.
techniques.signals: Narrow, low-volume impersonation runs.
techniques.defense: Cross-tenant intelligence sharing and DMARC reporting.

techniques.name: Lookalike registration
techniques.description: A cousin domain is registered to resemble a trusted one (bridges SPM-ID-02).
techniques.signals: Recently registered homoglyph or typo domains of a brand.
techniques.defense: Lookalike and brand monitoring; defensive registrations.

techniques.name: Homoglyph / IDN abuse
techniques.description: Internationalised or look-alike characters imitate familiar letters.
techniques.signals: Non-ASCII characters imitating a known domain.
techniques.defense: Homoglyph normalisation and IDN display policies.

techniques.name: Subdomain & dangling abuse
techniques.description: A dormant subdomain or dangling DNS record is taken over to send.
techniques.signals: Authenticated mail from long-inactive subdomains.
techniques.defense: DNS hygiene; retire dangling records; DMARC visibility.

techniques.name: New-domain impersonation
techniques.description: A freshly registered domain poses as an established identity.
techniques.signals: Very low domain age paired with a trusted-brand pretext.
techniques.defense: Newly-registered-domain scoring and greylisting.

techniques.name: Header-from spoofing
techniques.description: The visible From header is forged to a trusted address.
techniques.signals: From address failing authentication or alignment.
techniques.defense: Enforce DMARC; flag unauthenticated From.

techniques.name: Display-name impersonation
techniques.description: The friendly name claims a trusted identity while the address does not match.
techniques.signals: A known name paired with an unfamiliar address.
techniques.defense: Header analysis and external-sender warning banners.

techniques.name: Reply-to redirection
techniques.description: A different reply-to quietly steers responses to the attacker.
techniques.signals: Reply-to diverging from the visible From.
techniques.defense: Flag reply-to and From mismatches.

techniques.name: Envelope-from spoofing
techniques.description: The hidden return-path is forged independent of the visible From.
techniques.signals: Return-path inconsistent with the From domain.
techniques.defense: Evaluate envelope and header alignment together.

techniques.name: Phishing enablement
techniques.description: The forged identity makes a phishing lure credible (bridges PHI).
techniques.signals: Impersonation paired with a credential-capture link.
techniques.defense: Combine identity checks with URL detonation.

techniques.name: BEC enablement
techniques.description: The forged identity underpins a payment-fraud request (bridges BEC).
techniques.signals: Impersonation of an executive or vendor around payments.
techniques.defense: Out-of-band verification of payment requests.

techniques.name: Brand damage & disinformation
techniques.description: Impersonation is used to mislead or harm a brand's audience.
techniques.signals: Forged communications contradicting official channels.
techniques.defense: Public reporting channels and DMARC enforcement.

techniques.name: Deliverability sabotage
techniques.description: Spoofing a domain degrades its sending reputation.
techniques.signals: A rise in spoofed mail harming legitimate delivery.
techniques.defense: DMARC enforcement and reputation monitoring.
